In [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), the **special theory of relativity**, or **special relativity** for short, is a scientific theory of the relationship between [space and time](/wiki/Spacetime "Spacetime"). In [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein")'s 1905 paper, ["On the Electrodynamics of Moving Bodies"](/wiki/Annus_Mirabilis_papers#Special_relativity "Annus Mirabilis papers"), the theory is presented as being based on just [two postulates](/wiki/Postulates_of_special_relativity "Postulates of special relativity"):[\[p 1\]](#cite_note-electro-1)[\[1\]](#cite_note-Griffiths-2013-2)[\[2\]](#cite_note-Jackson-1999-3)

1.  The [laws of physics](/wiki/Laws_of_physics "Laws of physics") are [invariant](/wiki/Invariant_\(physics\) "Invariant (physics)") (identical) in all [inertial frames of reference](/wiki/Inertial_frame_of_reference "Inertial frame of reference") (that is, [frames of reference](/wiki/Frame_of_reference "Frame of reference") with no [acceleration](/wiki/Acceleration "Acceleration")). This is known as the [principle of relativity](/wiki/Principle_of_relativity "Principle of relativity").
2.  The [speed of light](/wiki/Speed_of_light "Speed of light") in [vacuum](/wiki/Vacuum "Vacuum") is the same for all observers, regardless of the motion of light source or observer. This is known as the principle of light constancy, or the principle of light speed invariance.

The first postulate was first formulated by [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei") (see *[Galilean invariance](/wiki/Galilean_invariance "Galilean invariance")*).

## Overview

\[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Special_relativity&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")\]

Relativity is a theory that accurately describes objects moving at speeds far beyond normal experience. Relativity replaces the idea that time flows equally everywhere in the universe with a new concept that time flows differently for every independent object. The flow of time can be expressed by counting ticks on a clock. Moving clocks run slower. Two events measured at the same time on a stationary clock occur at different times if measured on moving clocks. At speeds encountered in normal experience, the slow down cannot be observed. Near the speed of light the slow down is significant. At these relativistic speeds, many other physical effects can only be understood by including the effects of special relativity.[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4)

### Basis

\[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Special_relativity&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Basis")\]

Unusual among modern topics in physics, the theory of special relativity needs only mathematics at high school level and yet it fundamentally alters our understanding, especially our understanding of the concept of [time](/wiki/Time "Time").[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4): ix  Built on just two postulates or assumptions, many interesting consequences follow.

The two postulates both concern observers moving at a constant speed relative to each other. The first postulate, the [principle of relativity](#Principle_of_relativity), says the laws of physics do not depend on objects being at absolute rest: for example, an observer on a train sees natural phenomena on that train that look the same whether the train is moving or not.[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4): 5  The second postulate, constant speed of light, says observers in a train station see light travel at the same speed whether they measure light from within the station or light from a moving train. A light signal from the station to the train has the same speed, no matter how fast a train goes.[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4): 25 

In the theory of special relativity, the two postulates combine to change the definition of "relative speed". Rather than the simple concept of distance traveled divided by time spent, the new theory incorporates the speed of light as the maximum possible speed. In special relativity, covering ten times more distance on the ground in the same amount of time according to a moving watch does not result in a speed up as seen from the ground by a factor of ten.[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4): 28 

### Consequences

\[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Special_relativity&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Consequences")\]

Special relativity has a wide range of consequences that have been experimentally verified.[\[4\]](#cite_note-5)[\[5\]](#cite_note-6) The conceptual effects include:

-   The [relativity of simultaneity](#Relativity_of_simultaneity) – events that appear simultaneous to one observer may not be simultaneous to an observer in motion[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4): 49 
-   [§ Time dilation](#Time_dilation) – time measured between two events by observers in motion differ
-   [§ Length contraction](#Length_contraction) – distances between two events by observers in motion differ
-   The [§ Lorentz transformation of velocities](#Lorentz_transformation_of_velocities) – velocities no longer simply add

Combined with other laws of physics, the two postulates of special relativity predict the equivalence of [mass](/wiki/Mass "Mass") and [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy"), as expressed in the [mass–energy equivalence](/wiki/Mass%E2%80%93energy_equivalence "Mass–energy equivalence") formula ⁠ E \= m c 2 {\\displaystyle E=mc^{2}} ![{\displaystyle E=mc^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9f73dbd37a0cac34406ee89057fa1b36a1e6a18e) ⁠, where c {\\displaystyle c} ![{\displaystyle c}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/86a67b81c2de995bd608d5b2df50cd8cd7d92455) is the [speed of light](/wiki/Speed_of_light "Speed of light") in vacuum.[\[6\]](#cite_note-relativity-7)[\[7\]](#cite_note-Feynman-8) Special relativity replaced the conventional notion of an absolute, universal time with the notion of a time that is local to each observer.[\[8\]](#cite_note-9): 33  Information about distant objects can arrive no faster than the speed of light so visual observations always report events that have happened in the past. This effect makes visual descriptions of the effects of special relativity especially prone to mistakes.[\[9\]](#cite_note-10)

Special relativity also has profound technical consequences. A defining feature of special relativity is the replacement of [Euclidean geometry](/wiki/Euclidean_geometry "Euclidean geometry") with [Lorentzian geometry](/wiki/Lorentzian_geometry "Lorentzian geometry").[\[10\]](#cite_note-Taylor1992-11): 8  Distances in Euclidean geometry are calculated with the [Pythagorean theorem](/wiki/Pythagorean_theorem "Pythagorean theorem") and only involved spatial coordinates. In Lorentzian geometry, 'distances' become 'intervals' and include a time coordinate with a minus sign. Unlike spatial distances, the interval between two events has the same value for all observers independent of their relative velocity. When comparing two sets of coordinates in relative motion the [Lorentz transformation](/wiki/Lorentz_transformation "Lorentz transformation") replaces the [Galilean transformation](/wiki/Galilean_transformation "Galilean transformation") of Newtonian mechanics.[\[10\]](#cite_note-Taylor1992-11): 98  Other effects include the relativistic corrections to the [Doppler effect](/wiki/Doppler_effect "Doppler effect") and the [Thomas precession](/wiki/Thomas_precession "Thomas precession").[\[1\]](#cite_note-Griffiths-2013-2)[\[2\]](#cite_note-Jackson-1999-3) It also explains how electricity and magnetism are related.[\[1\]](#cite_note-Griffiths-2013-2)[\[2\]](#cite_note-Jackson-1999-3)

## History

\[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Special_relativity&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")\]

Main article: [History of special relativity](/wiki/History_of_special_relativity "History of special relativity")

The principle of relativity, forming one of the two postulates of special relativity, was described by [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei") in 1632 using a thought experiment involving observing natural phenomena on a moving ship.[\[11\]](#cite_note-12) His conclusions were summarized as [Galilean relativity](/wiki/Galilean_relativity "Galilean relativity") and used as the basis of [Newtonian mechanics](/wiki/Newtonian_mechanics "Newtonian mechanics").[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4): 1  This principle can be expressed as a [coordinate transformation](/wiki/Coordinate_transformation "Coordinate transformation"), between two coordinate systems. [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") noted that many transformations, such as those involving rotation or acceleration, will not preserve the observation of physical phenomena. Newton considered only those transformations involving motion with respect to an immovable absolute space, now called transformations between inertial frames.[\[12\]](#cite_note-Weinberg-1972-13): 17 

In 1864 [James Clerk Maxwell](/wiki/James_Clerk_Maxwell "James Clerk Maxwell") presented a theory of [electromagnetism](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism") which did not obey Galilean relativity. The theory specifically predicted a constant speed of light in vacuum, no matter the motion (velocity, acceleration, etc.) of the light emitter or receiver or its frequency, wavelength, direction, polarization, or phase. This, as yet untested theory, was thought at the time to be only valid in inertial frames fixed in an [aether](/wiki/Luminiferous_aether "Luminiferous aether"). Numerous experiments followed, attempting to measure the speed of light as Earth moved through the proposed fixed aether, culminating in the 1887 [Michelson–Morley experiment](/wiki/Michelson%E2%80%93Morley_experiment "Michelson–Morley experiment") which only confirmed the constant speed of light.[\[12\]](#cite_note-Weinberg-1972-13): 18 

Several fixes to the aether theory were proposed, with those of [George Francis FitzGerald](/wiki/George_Francis_FitzGerald "George Francis FitzGerald"), [Hendrik Antoon Lorentz](/wiki/Hendrik_Antoon_Lorentz "Hendrik Antoon Lorentz"), and [Jules Henri Poincare](/wiki/Jules_Henri_Poincare "Jules Henri Poincare") all pointing in the direction of a result similar to the theory of special relativity. The final important step was taken by Albert Einstein in a paper published on 26 September 1905 titled "On the Electrodynamics of Moving Bodies".[\[p 1\]](#cite_note-electro-1) Einstein applied the Lorentz transformations known to be compatible with [Maxwell's equations](/wiki/Maxwell%27s_equations "Maxwell's equations") for electrodynamics to the classical laws of mechanics. This changed Newton's mechanics situations involving all motions, especially velocities close to that of light[\[12\]](#cite_note-Weinberg-1972-13): 18  (known as *relativistic velocities*).

Another way to describe the advance made by the special theory is to say Einstein extended the Galilean principle so that it accounted for the constant speed of light,[\[10\]](#cite_note-Taylor1992-11) a phenomenon that had been observed in the Michelson–Morley experiment. He also postulated that it holds for all the [laws of physics](/wiki/Laws_of_physics "Laws of physics"), including both the laws of mechanics and of [electrodynamics](/wiki/Electrodynamics "Electrodynamics").[\[13\]](#cite_note-Rindler0-14) The theory became essentially complete in 1907, with [Hermann Minkowski](/wiki/Hermann_Minkowski "Hermann Minkowski")'s papers on spacetime.[\[14\]](#cite_note-Lanczos-1970-15)

Special relativity has proven to be the most accurate model of motion at any speed when gravitational and quantum effects are negligible.[\[15\]](#cite_note-16)[\[14\]](#cite_note-Lanczos-1970-15) Even so, the Newtonian model remains accurate at low velocities relative to the speed of light, for example, everyday motion on Earth.

In comparing to the general theory, Einstein specifically called his earlier work "special theory of relativity" (German: Spezielle Relativitätstheorie) in two short papers published in November 1915[\[16\]](#cite_note-17)[\[17\]](#cite_note-18) and in a long review article published in 1916,[\[18\]](#cite_note-19) saying he meant a restriction to frames in uniform motion, and was featured in the title of Einstein's popular book [Relativity: The Special and the General Theory](/wiki/Relativity:_The_Special_and_the_General_Theory "Relativity: The Special and the General Theory") first published in 1916. Just as [Galilean relativity](/wiki/Galilean_invariance "Galilean invariance") is accepted as an approximation of special relativity that is valid for low speeds, special relativity is considered an approximation of general relativity that is valid for weak [gravitational fields](/wiki/Gravitational_field "Gravitational field"), that is, at a sufficiently small scale (e.g., when [tidal forces](/wiki/Tidal_force "Tidal force") are negligible) and in conditions of [free fall](/wiki/Free_fall "Free fall"). But general relativity incorporates [non-Euclidean geometry](/wiki/Non-Euclidean_geometry "Non-Euclidean geometry") to represent gravitational effects as the geometric curvature of spacetime. Special relativity is restricted to the flat spacetime known as [Minkowski space](/wiki/Minkowski_space "Minkowski space"). As long as the universe can be modeled as a [pseudo-Riemannian manifold](/wiki/Pseudo-Riemannian_manifold "Pseudo-Riemannian manifold"), a Lorentz-invariant frame that abides by special relativity can be defined for a sufficiently small neighborhood of each point in this [curved spacetime](/wiki/Curved_spacetime "Curved spacetime").

In [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), the **special theory of relativity**, or **special relativity** for short, is a scientific theory of the relationship between [space and time](/wiki/Spacetime "Spacetime"). In [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein")'s 1905 paper, ["On the Electrodynamics of Moving Bodies"](/wiki/Annus_Mirabilis_papers#Special_relativity "Annus Mirabilis papers"), the theory is presented as being based on just [two postulates](/wiki/Postulates_of_special_relativity "Postulates of special relativity"):[\[p 1\]](#cite_note-electro-1)[\[1\]](#cite_note-Griffiths-2013-2)[\[2\]](#cite_note-Jackson-1999-3)

1.  The [laws of physics](/wiki/Laws_of_physics "Laws of physics") are [invariant](/wiki/Invariant_\(physics\) "Invariant (physics)") (identical) in all [inertial frames of reference](/wiki/Inertial_frame_of_reference "Inertial frame of reference") (that is, [frames of reference](/wiki/Frame_of_reference "Frame of reference") with no [acceleration](/wiki/Acceleration "Acceleration")). This is known as the [principle of relativity](/wiki/Principle_of_relativity "Principle of relativity").
2.  The [speed of light](/wiki/Speed_of_light "Speed of light") in [vacuum](/wiki/Vacuum "Vacuum") is the same for all observers, regardless of the motion of light source or observer. This is known as the principle of light constancy, or the principle of light speed invariance.

The first postulate was first formulated by [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei") (see *[Galilean invariance](/wiki/Galilean_invariance "Galilean invariance")*).

## Overview

\[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Special_relativity&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")\]

Relativity is a theory that accurately describes objects moving at speeds far beyond normal experience. Relativity replaces the idea that time flows equally everywhere in the universe with a new concept that time flows differently for every independent object. The flow of time can be expressed by counting ticks on a clock. Moving clocks run slower. Two events measured at the same time on a stationary clock occur at different times if measured on moving clocks. At speeds encountered in normal experience, the slow down cannot be observed. Near the speed of light the slow down is significant. At these relativistic speeds, many other physical effects can only be understood by including the effects of special relativity.[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4)

### Basis

\[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Special_relativity&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Basis")\]

Unusual among modern topics in physics, the theory of special relativity needs only mathematics at high school level and yet it fundamentally alters our understanding, especially our understanding of the concept of [time](/wiki/Time "Time").[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4): ix  Built on just two postulates or assumptions, many interesting consequences follow.

The two postulates both concern observers moving at a constant speed relative to each other. The first postulate, the [principle of relativity](#Principle_of_relativity), says the laws of physics do not depend on objects being at absolute rest: for example, an observer on a train sees natural phenomena on that train that look the same whether the train is moving or not.[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4): 5  The second postulate, constant speed of light, says observers in a train station see light travel at the same speed whether they measure light from within the station or light from a moving train. A light signal from the station to the train has the same speed, no matter how fast a train goes.[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4): 25 

In the theory of special relativity, the two postulates combine to change the definition of "relative speed". Rather than the simple concept of distance traveled divided by time spent, the new theory incorporates the speed of light as the maximum possible speed. In special relativity, covering ten times more distance on the ground in the same amount of time according to a moving watch does not result in a speed up as seen from the ground by a factor of ten.[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4): 28 

### Consequences

\[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Special_relativity&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Consequences")\]

Special relativity has a wide range of consequences that have been experimentally verified.[\[4\]](#cite_note-5)[\[5\]](#cite_note-6) The conceptual effects include:

-   The [relativity of simultaneity](#Relativity_of_simultaneity) – events that appear simultaneous to one observer may not be simultaneous to an observer in motion[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4): 49 
-   [§ Time dilation](#Time_dilation) – time measured between two events by observers in motion differ
-   [§ Length contraction](#Length_contraction) – distances between two events by observers in motion differ
-   The [§ Lorentz transformation of velocities](#Lorentz_transformation_of_velocities) – velocities no longer simply add

Combined with other laws of physics, the two postulates of special relativity predict the equivalence of [mass](/wiki/Mass "Mass") and [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy"), as expressed in the [mass–energy equivalence](/wiki/Mass%E2%80%93energy_equivalence "Mass–energy equivalence") formula ⁠ E \= m c 2 {\\displaystyle E=mc^{2}} ![{\displaystyle E=mc^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9f73dbd37a0cac34406ee89057fa1b36a1e6a18e) ⁠, where c {\\displaystyle c} ![{\displaystyle c}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/86a67b81c2de995bd608d5b2df50cd8cd7d92455) is the [speed of light](/wiki/Speed_of_light "Speed of light") in vacuum.[\[6\]](#cite_note-relativity-7)[\[7\]](#cite_note-Feynman-8) Special relativity replaced the conventional notion of an absolute, universal time with the notion of a time that is local to each observer.[\[8\]](#cite_note-9): 33  Information about distant objects can arrive no faster than the speed of light so visual observations always report events that have happened in the past. This effect makes visual descriptions of the effects of special relativity especially prone to mistakes.[\[9\]](#cite_note-10)

Special relativity also has profound technical consequences. A defining feature of special relativity is the replacement of [Euclidean geometry](/wiki/Euclidean_geometry "Euclidean geometry") with [Lorentzian geometry](/wiki/Lorentzian_geometry "Lorentzian geometry").[\[10\]](#cite_note-Taylor1992-11): 8  Distances in Euclidean geometry are calculated with the [Pythagorean theorem](/wiki/Pythagorean_theorem "Pythagorean theorem") and only involved spatial coordinates. In Lorentzian geometry, 'distances' become 'intervals' and include a time coordinate with a minus sign. Unlike spatial distances, the interval between two events has the same value for all observers independent of their relative velocity. When comparing two sets of coordinates in relative motion the [Lorentz transformation](/wiki/Lorentz_transformation "Lorentz transformation") replaces the [Galilean transformation](/wiki/Galilean_transformation "Galilean transformation") of Newtonian mechanics.[\[10\]](#cite_note-Taylor1992-11): 98  Other effects include the relativistic corrections to the [Doppler effect](/wiki/Doppler_effect "Doppler effect") and the [Thomas precession](/wiki/Thomas_precession "Thomas precession").[\[1\]](#cite_note-Griffiths-2013-2)[\[2\]](#cite_note-Jackson-1999-3) It also explains how electricity and magnetism are related.[\[1\]](#cite_note-Griffiths-2013-2)[\[2\]](#cite_note-Jackson-1999-3)

## History

\[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Special_relativity&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")\]

Main article: [History of special relativity](/wiki/History_of_special_relativity "History of special relativity")

The principle of relativity, forming one of the two postulates of special relativity, was described by [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei") in 1632 using a thought experiment involving observing natural phenomena on a moving ship.[\[11\]](#cite_note-12) His conclusions were summarized as [Galilean relativity](/wiki/Galilean_relativity "Galilean relativity") and used as the basis of [Newtonian mechanics](/wiki/Newtonian_mechanics "Newtonian mechanics").[\[3\]](#cite_note-Mermin-2009-4): 1  This principle can be expressed as a [coordinate transformation](/wiki/Coordinate_transformation "Coordinate transformation"), between two coordinate systems. [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") noted that many transformations, such as those involving rotation or acceleration, will not preserve the observation of physical phenomena. Newton considered only those transformations involving motion with respect to an immovable absolute space, now called transformations between inertial frames.[\[12\]](#cite_note-Weinberg-1972-13): 17 

In 1864 [James Clerk Maxwell](/wiki/James_Clerk_Maxwell "James Clerk Maxwell") presented a theory of [electromagnetism](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism") which did not obey Galilean relativity. The theory specifically predicted a constant speed of light in vacuum, no matter the motion (velocity, acceleration, etc.) of the light emitter or receiver or its frequency, wavelength, direction, polarization, or phase. This, as yet untested theory, was thought at the time to be only valid in inertial frames fixed in an [aether](/wiki/Luminiferous_aether "Luminiferous aether"). Numerous experiments followed, attempting to measure the speed of light as Earth moved through the proposed fixed aether, culminating in the 1887 [Michelson–Morley experiment](/wiki/Michelson%E2%80%93Morley_experiment "Michelson–Morley experiment") which only confirmed the constant speed of light.[\[12\]](#cite_note-Weinberg-1972-13): 18 

Several fixes to the aether theory were proposed, with those of [George Francis FitzGerald](/wiki/George_Francis_FitzGerald "George Francis FitzGerald"), [Hendrik Antoon Lorentz](/wiki/Hendrik_Antoon_Lorentz "Hendrik Antoon Lorentz"), and [Jules Henri Poincare](/wiki/Jules_Henri_Poincare "Jules Henri Poincare") all pointing in the direction of a result similar to the theory of special relativity. The final important step was taken by Albert Einstein in a paper published on 26 September 1905 titled "On the Electrodynamics of Moving Bodies".[\[p 1\]](#cite_note-electro-1) Einstein applied the Lorentz transformations known to be compatible with [Maxwell's equations](/wiki/Maxwell%27s_equations "Maxwell's equations") for electrodynamics to the classical laws of mechanics. This changed Newton's mechanics situations involving all motions, especially velocities close to that of light[\[12\]](#cite_note-Weinberg-1972-13): 18  (known as *relativistic velocities*).

Another way to describe the advance made by the special theory is to say Einstein extended the Galilean principle so that it accounted for the constant speed of light,[\[10\]](#cite_note-Taylor1992-11) a phenomenon that had been observed in the Michelson–Morley experiment. He also postulated that it holds for all the [laws of physics](/wiki/Laws_of_physics "Laws of physics"), including both the laws of mechanics and of [electrodynamics](/wiki/Electrodynamics "Electrodynamics").[\[13\]](#cite_note-Rindler0-14) The theory became essentially complete in 1907, with [Hermann Minkowski](/wiki/Hermann_Minkowski "Hermann Minkowski")'s papers on spacetime.[\[14\]](#cite_note-Lanczos-1970-15)

Special relativity has proven to be the most accurate model of motion at any speed when gravitational and quantum effects are negligible.[\[15\]](#cite_note-16)[\[14\]](#cite_note-Lanczos-1970-15) Even so, the Newtonian model remains accurate at low velocities relative to the speed of light, for example, everyday motion on Earth.

In comparing to the general theory, Einstein specifically called his earlier work "special theory of relativity" (German: Spezielle Relativitätstheorie) in two short papers published in November 1915[\[16\]](#cite_note-17)[\[17\]](#cite_note-18) and in a long review article published in 1916,[\[18\]](#cite_note-19) saying he meant a restriction to frames in uniform motion, and was featured in the title of Einstein's popular book [Relativity: The Special and the General Theory](/wiki/Relativity:_The_Special_and_the_General_Theory "Relativity: The Special and the General Theory") first published in 1916. Just as [Galilean relativity](/wiki/Galilean_invariance "Galilean invariance") is accepted as an approximation of special relativity that is valid for low speeds, special relativity is considered an approximation of general relativity that is valid for weak [gravitational fields](/wiki/Gravitational_field "Gravitational field"), that is, at a sufficiently small scale (e.g., when [tidal forces](/wiki/Tidal_force "Tidal force") are negligible) and in conditions of [free fall](/wiki/Free_fall "Free fall"). But general relativity incorporates [non-Euclidean geometry](/wiki/Non-Euclidean_geometry "Non-Euclidean geometry") to represent gravitational effects as the geometric curvature of spacetime. Special relativity is restricted to the flat spacetime known as [Minkowski space](/wiki/Minkowski_space "Minkowski space"). As long as the universe can be modeled as a [pseudo-Riemannian manifold](/wiki/Pseudo-Riemannian_manifold "Pseudo-Riemannian manifold"), a Lorentz-invariant frame that abides by special relativity can be defined for a sufficiently small neighborhood of each point in this [curved spacetime](/wiki/Curved_spacetime "Curved spacetime").

For a more accessible and less technical introduction to this topic, see [Introduction to general relativity](/wiki/Introduction_to_general_relativity "Introduction to general relativity").

Slow motion computer simulation of the black hole [binary system](/wiki/Binary_system "Binary system") GW150914 as seen by a nearby observer, during 0.33 s of its final [inspiral](/wiki/Binary_black_hole#Inspiral "Binary black hole"), [merge](/wiki/Binary_black_hole#Merger "Binary black hole"), and [ringdown](/wiki/Binary_black_hole#Ringdown "Binary black hole"). The star field behind the black holes is being heavily distorted and appears to rotate and move, due to extreme [gravitational lensing](/wiki/Gravitational_lens "Gravitational lens"), as [spacetime](/wiki/Spacetime "Spacetime") itself is distorted and dragged around by the rotating [black holes](/wiki/Black_hole "Black hole").[\[1\]](#cite_note-SXSproject-1)

**General relativity**, also known as the **general theory of relativity**, and as **Einstein's theory of gravity**, is the [geometric](/wiki/Differential_geometry "Differential geometry") theory of [gravitation](/wiki/Gravitation "Gravitation") published by [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein") in May 1916 and is the accepted description of the gravitation of macroscopic objects in [modern physics](/wiki/Modern_physics "Modern physics"). General [relativity](/wiki/Theory_of_relativity "Theory of relativity") generalizes [special relativity](/wiki/Special_relativity "Special relativity") and refines [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton")'s [law of universal gravitation](/wiki/Law_of_universal_gravitation "Law of universal gravitation"), providing a unified description of gravity as a geometric property of [space](/wiki/Space "Space") and [time](/wiki/Time_in_physics "Time in physics"), or four-dimensional [spacetime](/wiki/Spacetime "Spacetime"). In particular, the *[curvature](/wiki/Curvature "Curvature") of spacetime* is directly related to the [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy"), [momentum](/wiki/Momentum "Momentum"), and [stress](/wiki/Stress_\(mechanics\) "Stress (mechanics)") of whatever is present, including [matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter") and [radiation](/wiki/Radiation "Radiation"). The relation is specified by the [Einstein field equations](/wiki/Einstein_field_equations "Einstein field equations"), a system of second-order [partial differential equations](/wiki/Partial_differential_equation "Partial differential equation"). [John Archibald Wheeler](/wiki/John_Archibald_Wheeler "John Archibald Wheeler") summarized it: "Space-time tells matter how to move; matter tells space-time how to curve."

Newton's law of universal gravitation, which describes gravity in classical mechanics, can be seen as a prediction of general relativity for the almost flat spacetime geometry around stationary mass distributions. Some predictions of general relativity, however, are beyond Newton's law of universal gravitation in [classical physics](/wiki/Classical_physics "Classical physics"). These predictions concern the passage of time, the [geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry") of space, the motion of bodies in [free fall](/wiki/Free_fall "Free fall"), and the propagation of light, and include [gravitational time dilation](/wiki/Gravitational_time_dilation "Gravitational time dilation"), [gravitational lensing](/wiki/Gravitational_lens "Gravitational lens"), the [gravitational redshift](/wiki/Gravitational_redshift "Gravitational redshift") of light, the [Shapiro time delay](/wiki/Shapiro_time_delay "Shapiro time delay"), [singularities](/wiki/Gravitational_singularity "Gravitational singularity") and [black holes](/wiki/Black_hole "Black hole"). So far, all [tests of general relativity](/wiki/Tests_of_general_relativity "Tests of general relativity") have been in agreement with the theory. The time-dependent solutions of general relativity enable us to extrapolate the history of the universe into the past and future, and have provided the modern framework for [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology"), thus leading to the discovery of the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang") and [cosmic microwave background](/wiki/Cosmic_microwave_background "Cosmic microwave background") radiation. Despite the introduction of a number of [alternative theories](/wiki/Alternatives_to_general_relativity "Alternatives to general relativity"), general relativity continues to be the simplest theory consistent with [experimental data](/wiki/Experimental_data "Experimental data").

Reconciliation of general relativity with the laws of [quantum physics](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics") remains a problem, however, as no self-consistent theory of [quantum gravity](/wiki/Quantum_gravity "Quantum gravity") has been found. It is not yet known how gravity can be [unified](/wiki/Theory_of_everything "Theory of everything") with the three non-gravitational interactions: [strong](/wiki/Strong_interaction "Strong interaction"), [weak](/wiki/Weak_interaction "Weak interaction") and [electromagnetic](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism").

Einstein's theory has [astrophysical](/wiki/Astrophysical "Astrophysical") implications, including the prediction of [black holes](/wiki/Black_hole "Black hole")—regions of space in which space and time are distorted in such a way that nothing, not even [light](/wiki/Photon "Photon"), can escape from them. Black holes are the end-state for [massive stars](/wiki/Massive_star "Massive star"). [Microquasars](/wiki/Microquasar "Microquasar") and [active galactic nuclei](/wiki/Active_galactic_nucleus "Active galactic nucleus") are believed to be [stellar black holes](/wiki/Stellar_black_hole "Stellar black hole") and [supermassive black holes](/wiki/Supermassive_black_hole "Supermassive black hole"). It also predicts [gravitational lensing](/wiki/Gravitational_lensing "Gravitational lensing"), where the bending of light results in distorted and multiple images of the same distant astronomical phenomenon. Other predictions include the existence of [gravitational waves](/wiki/Gravitational_wave "Gravitational wave"), which have been [observed directly](/wiki/List_of_gravitational_wave_observations "List of gravitational wave observations") by the physics collaboration [LIGO](/wiki/LIGO "LIGO") and other observatories. In addition, general relativity has provided the basis for [cosmological](/wiki/Physical_cosmology "Physical cosmology") models of an [expanding universe](/wiki/Metric_expansion_of_space "Metric expansion of space").

Widely acknowledged as a theory of extraordinary [mathematical beauty](/wiki/Mathematical_beauty "Mathematical beauty"), general relativity has often been described as the most beautiful of all existing physical theories.[\[2\]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## History

\[[edit](/w/index.php?title=General_relativity&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")\]

Main articles: [History of general relativity](/wiki/History_of_general_relativity "History of general relativity") and [Classical theories of gravitation](/wiki/Classical_theories_of_gravitation "Classical theories of gravitation")

[Henri Poincaré](/wiki/Henri_Poincar%C3%A9 "Henri Poincaré")'s 1905 theory of the dynamics of the electron was a relativistic theory which he applied to all forces, including gravity. While others thought that gravity was instantaneous or of electromagnetic origin, he suggested that relativity was "something due to our methods of measurement". In his theory, he showed that [gravitational waves](/wiki/Gravitational_waves "Gravitational waves") propagate at the [speed of light](/wiki/Speed_of_light "Speed of light").[\[3\]](#cite_note-3) Soon afterwards, Einstein started thinking about how to incorporate [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") into his relativistic framework. In 1907, beginning with a simple [thought experiment](/wiki/Thought_experiment "Thought experiment") involving an observer in free fall (FFO), he embarked on what would be an eight-year search for a relativistic theory of gravity. After numerous detours and false starts, his work culminated in the presentation to the [Prussian Academy of Science](/wiki/Prussian_Academy_of_Science "Prussian Academy of Science") in November 1915 of what are known as the Einstein field equations, which form the core of Einstein's general theory of relativity.[\[4\]](#cite_note-4) These equations specify how the geometry of space and time is influenced by whatever matter and radiation are present.[\[5\]](#cite_note-5) A version of [non-Euclidean geometry](/wiki/Non-Euclidean_geometry "Non-Euclidean geometry"), called [Riemannian geometry](/wiki/Riemannian_geometry "Riemannian geometry"), enabled Einstein to develop general relativity by providing the key mathematical framework on which he fit his physical ideas of gravity.[\[6\]](#cite_note-6) This idea was pointed out by mathematician [Marcel Grossmann](/wiki/Marcel_Grossmann "Marcel Grossmann") and published by Grossmann and Einstein in 1913.[\[7\]](#cite_note-7)

The Einstein field equations are [nonlinear](/wiki/Nonlinear_differential_equation "Nonlinear differential equation") and are considered difficult to solve. Einstein used approximation methods in working out initial predictions of the theory. But in 1916, the astrophysicist [Karl Schwarzschild](/wiki/Karl_Schwarzschild "Karl Schwarzschild") found the first non-trivial exact solution to the Einstein field equations, the [Schwarzschild metric](/wiki/Schwarzschild_metric "Schwarzschild metric"). This solution laid the groundwork for the description of the final stages of gravitational collapse, and the objects known today as black holes. In the same year, the first steps towards generalizing Schwarzschild's solution to [electrically charged](/wiki/Electrical_charge "Electrical charge") objects were taken, eventually resulting in the [Reissner–Nordström solution](/wiki/Reissner%E2%80%93Nordstr%C3%B6m_metric "Reissner–Nordström metric"), which is associated with [electrically charged black holes](/wiki/Charged_black_hole "Charged black hole").[\[8\]](#cite_note-8) In 1917, Einstein applied his theory to the [universe](/wiki/Universe "Universe") as a whole, initiating the field of relativistic cosmology. In line with contemporary thinking, he assumed a static universe, adding a new parameter to his original field equations—the [cosmological constant](/wiki/Cosmological_constant "Cosmological constant")—to match that observational presumption.[\[9\]](#cite_note-9) By 1929, however, the work of [Hubble](/wiki/Edwin_Hubble "Edwin Hubble") and others had shown that the universe is expanding. This is readily described by the expanding cosmological solutions found by [Friedmann](/wiki/Alexander_Friedmann "Alexander Friedmann") in 1922, which do not require a cosmological constant. [Lemaître](/wiki/Georges_Lema%C3%AEtre "Georges Lemaître") used these solutions to formulate the earliest version of the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang") models, in which the universe has evolved from an extremely hot and dense earlier state.[\[10\]](#cite_note-10) Einstein later declared the cosmological constant the biggest blunder of his life.[\[11\]](#cite_note-11)

**General relativity**, also known as the **general theory of relativity**, and as **Einstein's theory of gravity**, is the [geometric](/wiki/Differential_geometry "Differential geometry") theory of [gravitation](/wiki/Gravitation "Gravitation") published by [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein") in May 1916 and is the accepted description of the gravitation of macroscopic objects in [modern physics](/wiki/Modern_physics "Modern physics"). General [relativity](/wiki/Theory_of_relativity "Theory of relativity") generalizes [special relativity](/wiki/Special_relativity "Special relativity") and refines [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton")'s [law of universal gravitation](/wiki/Law_of_universal_gravitation "Law of universal gravitation"), providing a unified description of gravity as a geometric property of [space](/wiki/Space "Space") and [time](/wiki/Time_in_physics "Time in physics"), or four-dimensional [spacetime](/wiki/Spacetime "Spacetime"). In particular, the *[curvature](/wiki/Curvature "Curvature") of spacetime* is directly related to the [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy"), [momentum](/wiki/Momentum "Momentum"), and [stress](/wiki/Stress_\(mechanics\) "Stress (mechanics)") of whatever is present, including [matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter") and [radiation](/wiki/Radiation "Radiation"). The relation is specified by the [Einstein field equations](/wiki/Einstein_field_equations "Einstein field equations"), a system of second-order [partial differential equations](/wiki/Partial_differential_equation "Partial differential equation"). [John Archibald Wheeler](/wiki/John_Archibald_Wheeler "John Archibald Wheeler") summarized it: "Space-time tells matter how to move; matter tells space-time how to curve."

Newton's law of universal gravitation, which describes gravity in classical mechanics, can be seen as a prediction of general relativity for the almost flat spacetime geometry around stationary mass distributions. Some predictions of general relativity, however, are beyond Newton's law of universal gravitation in [classical physics](/wiki/Classical_physics "Classical physics"). These predictions concern the passage of time, the [geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry") of space, the motion of bodies in [free fall](/wiki/Free_fall "Free fall"), and the propagation of light, and include [gravitational time dilation](/wiki/Gravitational_time_dilation "Gravitational time dilation"), [gravitational lensing](/wiki/Gravitational_lens "Gravitational lens"), the [gravitational redshift](/wiki/Gravitational_redshift "Gravitational redshift") of light, the [Shapiro time delay](/wiki/Shapiro_time_delay "Shapiro time delay"), [singularities](/wiki/Gravitational_singularity "Gravitational singularity") and [black holes](/wiki/Black_hole "Black hole"). So far, all [tests of general relativity](/wiki/Tests_of_general_relativity "Tests of general relativity") have been in agreement with the theory. The time-dependent solutions of general relativity enable us to extrapolate the history of the universe into the past and future, and have provided the modern framework for [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology"), thus leading to the discovery of the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang") and [cosmic microwave background](/wiki/Cosmic_microwave_background "Cosmic microwave background") radiation. Despite the introduction of a number of [alternative theories](/wiki/Alternatives_to_general_relativity "Alternatives to general relativity"), general relativity continues to be the simplest theory consistent with [experimental data](/wiki/Experimental_data "Experimental data").

Reconciliation of general relativity with the laws of [quantum physics](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics") remains a problem, however, as no self-consistent theory of [quantum gravity](/wiki/Quantum_gravity "Quantum gravity") has been found. It is not yet known how gravity can be [unified](/wiki/Theory_of_everything "Theory of everything") with the three non-gravitational interactions: [strong](/wiki/Strong_interaction "Strong interaction"), [weak](/wiki/Weak_interaction "Weak interaction") and [electromagnetic](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism").

Einstein's theory has [astrophysical](/wiki/Astrophysical "Astrophysical") implications, including the prediction of [black holes](/wiki/Black_hole "Black hole")—regions of space in which space and time are distorted in such a way that nothing, not even [light](/wiki/Photon "Photon"), can escape from them. Black holes are the end-state for [massive stars](/wiki/Massive_star "Massive star"). [Microquasars](/wiki/Microquasar "Microquasar") and [active galactic nuclei](/wiki/Active_galactic_nucleus "Active galactic nucleus") are believed to be [stellar black holes](/wiki/Stellar_black_hole "Stellar black hole") and [supermassive black holes](/wiki/Supermassive_black_hole "Supermassive black hole"). It also predicts [gravitational lensing](/wiki/Gravitational_lensing "Gravitational lensing"), where the bending of light results in distorted and multiple images of the same distant astronomical phenomenon. Other predictions include the existence of [gravitational waves](/wiki/Gravitational_wave "Gravitational wave"), which have been [observed directly](/wiki/List_of_gravitational_wave_observations "List of gravitational wave observations") by the physics collaboration [LIGO](/wiki/LIGO "LIGO") and other observatories. In addition, general relativity has provided the basis for [cosmological](/wiki/Physical_cosmology "Physical cosmology") models of an [expanding universe](/wiki/Metric_expansion_of_space "Metric expansion of space").

Widely acknowledged as a theory of extraordinary [mathematical beauty](/wiki/Mathematical_beauty "Mathematical beauty"), general relativity has often been described as the most beautiful of all existing physical theories.[\[2\]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## History

\[[edit](/w/index.php?title=General_relativity&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")\]

Main articles: [History of general relativity](/wiki/History_of_general_relativity "History of general relativity") and [Classical theories of gravitation](/wiki/Classical_theories_of_gravitation "Classical theories of gravitation")

[Henri Poincaré](/wiki/Henri_Poincar%C3%A9 "Henri Poincaré")'s 1905 theory of the dynamics of the electron was a relativistic theory which he applied to all forces, including gravity. While others thought that gravity was instantaneous or of electromagnetic origin, he suggested that relativity was "something due to our methods of measurement". In his theory, he showed that [gravitational waves](/wiki/Gravitational_waves "Gravitational waves") propagate at the [speed of light](/wiki/Speed_of_light "Speed of light").[\[3\]](#cite_note-3) Soon afterwards, Einstein started thinking about how to incorporate [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") into his relativistic framework. In 1907, beginning with a simple [thought experiment](/wiki/Thought_experiment "Thought experiment") involving an observer in free fall (FFO), he embarked on what would be an eight-year search for a relativistic theory of gravity. After numerous detours and false starts, his work culminated in the presentation to the [Prussian Academy of Science](/wiki/Prussian_Academy_of_Science "Prussian Academy of Science") in November 1915 of what are known as the Einstein field equations, which form the core of Einstein's general theory of relativity.[\[4\]](#cite_note-4) These equations specify how the geometry of space and time is influenced by whatever matter and radiation are present.[\[5\]](#cite_note-5) A version of [non-Euclidean geometry](/wiki/Non-Euclidean_geometry "Non-Euclidean geometry"), called [Riemannian geometry](/wiki/Riemannian_geometry "Riemannian geometry"), enabled Einstein to develop general relativity by providing the key mathematical framework on which he fit his physical ideas of gravity.[\[6\]](#cite_note-6) This idea was pointed out by mathematician [Marcel Grossmann](/wiki/Marcel_Grossmann "Marcel Grossmann") and published by Grossmann and Einstein in 1913.[\[7\]](#cite_note-7)

The Einstein field equations are [nonlinear](/wiki/Nonlinear_differential_equation "Nonlinear differential equation") and are considered difficult to solve. Einstein used approximation methods in working out initial predictions of the theory. But in 1916, the astrophysicist [Karl Schwarzschild](/wiki/Karl_Schwarzschild "Karl Schwarzschild") found the first non-trivial exact solution to the Einstein field equations, the [Schwarzschild metric](/wiki/Schwarzschild_metric "Schwarzschild metric"). This solution laid the groundwork for the description of the final stages of gravitational collapse, and the objects known today as black holes. In the same year, the first steps towards generalizing Schwarzschild's solution to [electrically charged](/wiki/Electrical_charge "Electrical charge") objects were taken, eventually resulting in the [Reissner–Nordström solution](/wiki/Reissner%E2%80%93Nordstr%C3%B6m_metric "Reissner–Nordström metric"), which is associated with [electrically charged black holes](/wiki/Charged_black_hole "Charged black hole").[\[8\]](#cite_note-8) In 1917, Einstein applied his theory to the [universe](/wiki/Universe "Universe") as a whole, initiating the field of relativistic cosmology. In line with contemporary thinking, he assumed a static universe, adding a new parameter to his original field equations—the [cosmological constant](/wiki/Cosmological_constant "Cosmological constant")—to match that observational presumption.[\[9\]](#cite_note-9) By 1929, however, the work of [Hubble](/wiki/Edwin_Hubble "Edwin Hubble") and others had shown that the universe is expanding. This is readily described by the expanding cosmological solutions found by [Friedmann](/wiki/Alexander_Friedmann "Alexander Friedmann") in 1922, which do not require a cosmological constant. [Lemaître](/wiki/Georges_Lema%C3%AEtre "Georges Lemaître") used these solutions to formulate the earliest version of the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang") models, in which the universe has evolved from an extremely hot and dense earlier state.[\[10\]](#cite_note-10) Einstein later declared the cosmological constant the biggest blunder of his life.[\[11\]](#cite_note-11)

# The Theory of Relativity

The theory of relativity, developed by Albert Einstein, consists of two interconnected theories: **special relativity** (1905) and **general relativity** (1916

). Together, they revolutionized our understanding of space, time, and gravity.

---

## Special Relativity

### The Two Postulates

Special relativity is

built on just two fundamental postulates:

1. **Principle of Relativity**: The laws of physics are identical in all inertial frames of reference (non-accelerating frames). An observer on a moving train sees

the same natural phenomena as one at rest.

2. **Invariance of the Speed of Light**: The speed of light in vacuum (≈299,792 km/s) is constant for all observers,

regardless of the motion of the light source or observer.

### Key Consequences

**Time Dilation**: Moving clocks run slower than stationary ones. The time

elapsed between two events depends on the observer's frame of reference.

**Length Contraction**: Objects moving relative to an observer appear contracted in the

direction of motion.

**Relativity of Simultaneity**: Events that appear simultaneous to one observer may not be simultaneous to another observer in motion relative to the first.

**Mass-

Energy Equivalence**: E = mc² — mass and energy are equivalent and interchangeable.

### The Lorentz Transformation

At relativistic speeds, the

Galilean transformations of Newtonian mechanics are replaced by Lorentz transformations:

```
t' = γ(t - vx/c²)
x' = γ(x - vt)
```

where γ =

1/√(1 - v²/c²) is the Lorentz factor.

### Minkowski Spacetime

Hermann Minkowski unified space and time into a four-dimensional spacetime continuum

. Events are described by spacetime intervals rather than separate spatial and temporal distances.

---

## General Relativity

### The Core Idea

General
relativity describes gravity not as a force, but as the curvature of spacetime caused by mass and energy. As John Archibald Wheeler famously put

it: *"Space-time tells matter how to move; matter tells space-time how to curve."*

### The Equivalence Principle

A fundamental building block: the effects of
gravity are locally indistinguishable from acceleration. An observer in a closed room cannot tell whether they feel weight due to gravity or

due to uniform acceleration.

### Einstein Field Equations

The core of general relativity is given by:

```
Gμν + Λgμν =

κTμν
```

Where:
- Gμν is the Einstein tensor (curvature of spacetime)
- Λ is the cosmological constant
- gμν is the metric tensor
- Tμν is the

stress-energy tensor (matter and energy content)
- κ is a coupling constant

### Predictions and Phenomena

1. **Gravitational Time

Dilation**: Time runs slower in stronger gravitational fields (confirmed by GPS satellites)

2. **Gravitational Lensing**: Light bends

around massive objects, creating multiple images (observed during solar eclipses in 1919)

3. **Gravitational Redshift**: Light loses

energy climbing out of a gravitational well, shifting to redder wavelengths

4. **Gravitational Waves**: Ripples in spacetime caused by accelerating masses (directly detected by LIGO in 2015)

5. **Black H

oles**: Regions where spacetime curvature becomes so extreme that nothing, not even light, can escape

6. **Gravitational Time Delay (Sh

apiro Effect)**: Light takes longer to travel around massive objects

7. **Perihelion Precession of Mercury**: The orbit precesses more than

Newtonian mechanics predicts (43 arcseconds/century)

8. **Frame-Dragging**: Rotating masses

drag spacetime around with them (confirmed by Gravity Probe B)

### Cosmological Applications

General relativity enables models of an expanding universe,

predicting the Big Bang, cosmic microwave background radiation, and the accelerated expansion driven by dark energy.

## Experimental Verification

Both theories have been extensively tested

:

| Test | Evidence |
|------|----------|
| Michelson-Morley | No "a

ether" detected |
| Time dilation in particle decays | Confirmed in accelerators |
| E = mc² | Nuclear

energy, particle physics |
| Gravitational lensing | Galaxy cluster observations |
| Gravitational waves | L

IGO/Virgo detections |
| GPS satellite corrections | Essential for accuracy |

---

## Relationship Between

the Theories

- Special relativity applies to flat spacetime (no gravity)
- General relativity extends special relativity to include

gravity and curved spacetime
- Special relativity is the low-gravity, weak-field limit

of general relativity
- Reconciling general relativity with quantum mechanics remains an unsolved problem in physics
